perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure or use of the Confidential Information as it uses for its own confidential, proprietary and trade secret information, but in no case us e less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall use some degree of care to avoid disclosure or use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the 'reasonable degree of care' standard, potentially allowing for negligence in protecting confidential information. This introduces legal risk, as trade secrets require a higher standard of protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: MUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By eliminating the 'reasonable degree of care' standard, the modified text weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets under Michigan law, potentially making it easier to misappropriate trade secrets without liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'reasonable degree of care' standard, replacing it with 'some degree of care.' The Law Explanation states that MUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified contract weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, potentially making it easier to misappropriate trade secrets without liability. Scraped Law Snippet 1, MCL Section 445.1903, discusses misappropriation and injunctions related to trade secrets, indicating a legal emphasis on protecting trade secrets. The omission of the 'reasonable degree of care' standard directly contradicts the requirement of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall provide written notice to Disclosing Party without undue delay of any misuse or mis appropriation of Confidential Information which may come to the attention of Recipient .
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may provide written notice to Disclosing Party of any misuse or mis appropriation of Confidential Information which may come to the attention of Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall provide' to 'may provide' makes the notification of misuse optional, which conflicts with the duty to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: MUTSA implies a duty to take reasonable steps to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes notifying the disclosing party of any misuse. Making this notification optional undermines this duty and increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1903 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1903 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1903 Misappropriation; injunction. Sec. 3. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court of competent jurisdiction, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) If a court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use of a trade secret, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1998, Act 448 , Imd. Eff. Dec. 30, 1998
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall provide' to 'may provide' weakens the obligation to report misuse of confidential information. According to the law explanation, MUTSA implies a duty to take reasonable steps to prevent misappropriation, which includes notifying the disclosing party of misuse. Section 445.1903(3) states that 'affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order.' Making notification optional undermines the duty to protect trade secrets, creating a contradiction because the original contract mandated an action to protect trade secrets while the changed contract removes this mandate.

perturbation.original_text: Disclosing Party may serve written request on Recipient for return or destruction of its Confidential Information at any time up to six (6) months after the termination or expiry of this Agreement and Recipient shall, within thirty (30) days of such request or termination, return to the Disclosing Party (or its designees) or certify as destroyed all Confidential Information, in whatever form, including written or electronically recorded information and al l copies thereof (other than copies retained in automatic back -up and archive systems), provided however that Recipient shall be entitled to retain one copy of the Confidential Information with its legal counsel or other appropriate corporate representativ e to evidence the exchange of information hereunder and in connection with legal or statutory requirements .
perturbation.changed_text: Disclosing Party may request the Recipient to return its Confidential Information after the termination or expiry of this Agreement, and Recipient shall return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the specific timeframe (six months for the request, thirty days for the return), the allowance for retaining a copy with legal counsel for legal/statutory requirements, and the option to certify destruction. This significantly weakens the disclosing party's control over their confidential information post-agreement, especially concerning regulatory compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under MUTSA, trade secrets must be subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain their secrecy. By removing the specific timeframes for return/destruction and the exception for legal retention, the agreement becomes less enforceable in protecting trade secrets because there is no way for the disclosing party to assure destruction of the documents. By removing the possibility for destruction confirmation, the agreement fails to assure the disclosing party that their data is being protected and that the recipient is taking efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.1902 - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.1902 Download Section Chapter 445 Act 448 of 1998 Previous Section Next Section UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT (EXCERPT) Act 448 of 1998 445.1902 Definitions. Sec. 2. As used in this act: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach, or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or any other means. (b) "Misappropriation" means either of the following: (i) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. (ii) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who did 1 or more of the following: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means an individual, corporation, partnership, association, governmental entity, or any other legal entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that is both of the following: (i) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. (ii) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History: 1998, Act 44
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language contradicts the requirements of maintaining trade secret protection under the Michigan Uniform Trade Secrets Act (MUTSA). The original text outlined specific actions to protect confidential information, including timeframes for return/destruction and allowing retention of a copy for legal purposes. The changed text removes these safeguards. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines a 'trade secret' as information that 'is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' By eliminating the specific procedures for return or destruction and the allowance for legal retention, the modified agreement provides fewer assurances that the disclosing party's trade secrets are being reasonably protected, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If Recipient is requested , ordered or required by a regulatory agency or any other government authority or a court to disclose any Confidential Information, Recipient shall promptly notify Disclosing Party of such request , order or requirement so that Disclosing Part y may have the opportunity to contest the disclosure, including seek ing a protective order , or waive Recipient’s compliance with this Agreement. If Recipient is (in the opinion of its counsel ) compelled to disclose any Confidential Information , or else be liable for contempt or other penalty or be subject to claims from a third party , Recipient may disclose such Confidential Information without liability under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: If Recipient is requested by a regulatory agency or any other government authority or a court to disclose any Confidential Information, Recipient may disclose such Confidential Information without liability under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the requirement to promptly notify the disclosing party of a request for confidential information and omits any mention of the disclosing party having the opportunity to contest the disclosure or seek a protective order. It also removes the 'opinion of its counsel' clause. This prevents the disclosing party from taking steps to protect its confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: MCR 2.302(B) governs the scope of discovery in Michigan courts, allowing for protective orders to safeguard confidential information. While this rule doesn't mandate notification in every instance, the removal of the notification requirement and the opportunity to seek a protective order undermines the ability of the disclosing party to avail itself of this legal protection. By saying the recipient may disclose it without liability, regardless, makes it nearly impossible for the disclosing party to invoke Rule 2.302(B) since they will not know when the recipient is disclosing information.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the recipient's obligation to promptly notify the disclosing party when requested by a regulatory agency or court to disclose confidential information. It also eliminates the disclosing party's opportunity to contest the disclosure or seek a protective order. This omission directly contradicts the principles outlined in the Law Explanation, which highlights the importance of protective orders to safeguard confidential information under MCR 2.302(B). By removing the notification requirement and the opportunity to seek a protective order, the changed text undermines the ability of the disclosing party to protect its confidential information.
